architecture_layers.types: src/types.ts (139 lines) — 16 interfaces and type aliases defining every data structure
architecture_layers.core: src/core/ (6 files, 860 lines) — git.ts, config.ts, worktree.ts, setup.ts, health.ts, process.ts, shell.ts
architecture_layers.commands: src/commands/ (11 files, 435 lines) — create, go, list, status, setup, sync-env, clean, doctor, repair, exec, init
architecture_layers.cli: src/cli.ts (147 lines) — argument parsing, command dispatch, interactive menu
architecture_layers.ui: src/ui/ (2 files, 305 lines) — display.ts (formatting), prompts.ts (interactive menus)

source_analysis.architecture_summary: 3-layer CLI tool: types (data shapes) → core (business logic: git ops, config loading, worktree management, setup orchestration, health checks) → commands (CLI surface, one per action). cli.ts dispatches commands. The process.ts module is the foundational I/O layer that everything else builds on — it wraps child_process.spawnSync for both direct commands and shell commands.

steps.title: Types: structs and enums
steps.architecture_context: The types file is the skeleton of the entire project. Every other module imports from here. It defines 16 interfaces covering config shapes, git data, worktree info, health reports, and command context. Understanding these types = understanding what data flows through the tool.

steps.title: Process spawning: the I/O foundation
steps.architecture_context: process.ts is the lowest-level module — everything that talks to the outside world goes through here. It wraps Node's spawnSync into two functions: runProcess (direct commands) and runShell (shell strings). git.ts, setup.ts, and shell.ts all depend on it. Building this first means you have a working I/O layer before touching any git logic.

steps.title: Config: file I/O and serialization
steps.architecture_context: config.ts reads wt.config.json (or package.json#wt) from the project root, validates it against a schema (zod in TS), applies defaults for missing fields, and returns a LoadedConfig. Every command loads config as one of its first steps. This teaches you Rust's approach to file I/O + structured parsing — the serde ecosystem is one of Rust's killer features.

steps.title: Git operations: the command layer
steps.architecture_context: git.ts is the largest core module (193 lines). It wraps every git command the tool needs: checking if you're in a repo, listing branches, getting status, checking if branches are merged, fetching, finding the default branch. It's pure process.ts + string parsing. This is where you'll spend the most time translating line-by-line, and where Rust's string handling really differs from TypeScript.

steps.title: Worktree operations: combining git + config
rust_concepts: async functions and tokio (optional — can stay sync)
steps.architecture_context: worktree.ts is the heart of the tool. It combines git.ts operations to list, create, find, and remove worktrees. The parseWorktreeList function parses git's porcelain output into structured data. createWorktree handles three modes (local branch, remote branch, new branch). getCleanableWorktrees categorizes worktrees for cleanup. This is the first module where you're orchestrating multiple git operations together.

steps.title: CLI dispatch: clap and pattern matching
steps.architecture_context: cli.ts is the entry point. It parses command-line arguments, resolves aliases (c→create, ls→list, sw→go), and dispatches to the right command handler. If no command is given and we're in a TTY, it shows an interactive menu. This is where Rust's match expression absolutely shines — the TypeScript switch statement maps directly to idiomatic Rust.

steps.title: Command: list (read-only, simplest)
steps.architecture_context: The list command is the simplest — it calls getAllWorktrees and prints them in a formatted table. display.ts handles the formatting (colored output, table alignment). This is a good first command because it's read-only: no mutations, no error paths to handle beyond 'not a git repo'. You'll learn how to format and display data in Rust.

steps.title: Command: status (read-only, with config)
steps.architecture_context: status shows an overview of all worktrees with their git status, health, and last modified time. It loads config and combines worktree data with health data. Slightly more complex than list because it touches config + worktree + health, but still read-only.

steps.title: Command: create (write operations)
rust_concepts: error handling patterns (custom error types, thiserror)
steps.architecture_context: create is the first command that mutates state — it creates a new worktree (via worktree.ts) then runs setup on it. This introduces the pattern of 'do a thing, then verify it worked'. Error handling matters here because git operations can fail in many ways.

steps.title: Commands: doctor, repair, clean (health system)
steps.architecture_context: These three commands form the health system. doctor inspects and reports, repair fixes, clean removes. They share the health.ts module which categorizes issues (dirty, ahead, behind, stale, orphaned). This batch teaches you how to build a diagnostic/repair pipeline — a pattern that transfers to any tooling project.

steps.title: Commands: go, exec, init, sync-env (remaining commands)
steps.architecture_context: go prints or resolves a worktree path (used by shell integration). exec runs a command inside a worktree. init scaffolds a new wt.config.json. sync-env re-runs copy steps. These are the utility commands — each is small and focused, good for practicing what you've learned from the earlier steps.

steps.title: Shell integration and interactive menu
steps.architecture_context: shell.ts generates shell init scripts (zsh, bash, fish) that hook the wt command into the shell so 'wt go <branch>' actually cd's into the worktree. prompts.ts provides an interactive menu when you run wt with no arguments. These are the finishing touches that make the tool feel complete. Shell script generation is a legitimate Rust use case — you're producing strings that get eval'd by another language.
